q: In Foundry, what is the Ontology and how does it differ from a plain data warehouse schema?
a: The Ontology is an operational, semantic layer that maps integrated data to real-world entities: object types (schemas like Employee), objects (instances), and link types (relationships like worksFor). Unlike a warehouse schema, it also carries a kinetic layer: action types that write edits back, and functions that encode logic, so apps and AIP can both read and operate on the same model. It is a shared digital twin that powers operational apps, not just analytics.
fj: It's just Foundry's name for database tables and foreign keys.
fs: Objects, links, and properties model the world semantically, while action types and functions let you write edits back — so the Ontology is an operational layer, not a read-only schema.

followups.q: Where does the data backing an object type physically live — is the Ontology a separate database?
followups.a: Object types are backed by Foundry datasets (or restricted views/streams) that get indexed into object databases by the indexing layer. The Ontology is not a fresh copy-store; it's an index over your materialized pipeline outputs plus action edits. So the source of truth is still the dataset, and the object DB is a queryable, low-latency projection of it.

followups.q: If the Ontology is just an index over datasets, why not let apps query the datasets directly?
followups.a: Datasets are columnar batch files optimized for Spark scans, not point lookups or per-object writes. The object DB gives indexed, low-latency reads, link traversal, and governed `action` writeback that datasets can't. Querying datasets directly would mean no live edits, no relationship traversal, and full scans for single-object fetches.

followups.q: Can two object types share the same backing dataset, and why might you do that?
followups.a: Yes — multiple object types can be backed by the same dataset, typically when one physical table holds rows of different conceptual entities or you want different `primaryKey`/property mappings over the same data. It avoids duplicating pipelines, but you must ensure each type's primary key is unique within its row subset or indexing will collide.

q: Walk through the relationship between object types, objects, link types, and links.
a: An object type is the schema definition of a real-world entity or event; an object is a single instance of that type. A link type is the schema definition of a relationship between two object types; a link is a single instance connecting two specific objects. So Employee and Company are object types, Alice is an object, the worksFor link type defines the relation, and Alice-to-Acme is one link.
fj: Objects are rows, links are joins done at query time.
fs: Object types and link types are schemas; objects and links are instances of those schemas — and links are first-class modeled relationships, not joins you compute at query time.

followups.q: How is a many-to-many link type backed differently from a one-to-many?
followups.a: A one-to-many link is backed by a foreign-key property on the many side pointing at the one side's primary key. A many-to-many link needs a separate backing dataset — a join/mapping table whose rows each carry both primary keys. The link type points at that intermediary, which is why many-to-many requires you to materialize the pairing explicitly.

followups.q: If a linked object's primary key changes, what happens to existing links?
followups.a: Links resolve by primary-key value, so changing a key effectively breaks the link — the old key no longer matches and the relationship is orphaned. Primary keys should be stable, immutable identifiers. If keys must change, you re-key both the objects and every backing foreign-key/join column in the same release so links re-resolve correctly.

followups.q: Why model a relationship as a link type instead of just storing the related object's properties on one object?
followups.a: Flattening loses cardinality, traversal, and independent updates — you can't navigate from the other side, and one entity's change forces rewriting the other. Link types let either side traverse the relationship, keep each object's lifecycle independent, and support many-to-many. Denormalizing only makes sense for stable, single-valued attributes you never traverse from.

q: What is an action type in the Ontology and why route writes through it instead of editing a dataset?
a: An action type is the schema for a set of edits a user can apply atomically to objects, properties, and links, plus side effects like notifications or function calls. Actions enforce parameter validation, submission criteria, and permissions, and they record who changed what for an audit trail. Routing writes through actions gives governed, validated, attributable writeback into the Ontology rather than uncontrolled dataset mutation.
fj: An action is just a button that runs an UPDATE on the underlying table.
fs: An action type is the governed write API of the Ontology — it enforces validation rules, runs side effects, attributes every edit, and applies atomically — not a thin wrapper over a SQL update.

followups.q: An action's submission criteria block a valid-looking edit. How do submission criteria differ from parameter validation?
followups.a: Parameter validation (rules) checks the inputs in isolation — e.g., `qty > 0`. Submission criteria gate whether the action can be applied at all given the current object/user state — e.g., "only if Order.status is OPEN" or the user holds a role. Validation says the parameters are well-formed; submission criteria say this user may apply this edit to this object right now.

followups.q: Two users apply the same action to the same object concurrently. What guarantees consistency?
followups.a: Each action applies as an atomic edit against the object's current state, and the funnel/indexing layer serializes the writes, so one wins and the other applies on top or is re-evaluated against the updated state. Submission criteria are re-checked at apply time, so a stale precondition (e.g., already-closed order) causes the later action to fail rather than silently clobber.

followups.q: When is it better to express logic as an action's side-effect function call versus a separate downstream transform?
followups.a: Use a side-effect (function/notification) when the consequence must happen synchronously and atomically with the edit — e.g., notify on submit or compute a derived field at write time. Use a downstream transform when the work is heavy, batch, or analytical and doesn't need to be tied to the individual edit's transaction. Actions are for live, governed consequences; transforms for bulk reshaping.

q: A basic Python transform reads dataset A and writes dataset B. Sketch the decorator and signature.
a: You use the transforms-python API: decorate a function with @transform, declaring outputs via Output and inputs via Input, then read each input as a Spark DataFrame and write to the output. The function receives those handles as parameters and calls output.write_dataframe(df). @transform_df is sugar for the single-output case.
fj: You just write a PySpark script; Foundry figures out inputs and outputs from the code.
fs: Inputs and outputs must be declared explicitly in the decorator — that declaration is what Foundry registers in the build graph and produces lineage; arbitrary reads inside the function body are invisible to the pipeline.

followups.q: What does declaring inputs/outputs in the decorator give you that an arbitrary spark.read inside the function would not?
followups.a: The declared `Input`/`Output` paths are what Foundry registers in the build graph — they produce lineage, scheduling, and permission resolution. A raw read inside the body would fetch data but be invisible to the dependency graph, so Foundry couldn't know to rebuild this transform when that source changes. Declaration is what makes the pipeline orchestratable, not just runnable.

followups.q: When would you use @transform over the @transform_df sugar?
followups.a: Use `@transform` when you need multiple outputs, access to transaction metadata, custom write modes, or to control the write call (e.g., partitioning, incremental modes). `@transform_df` is the single-input/single-output convenience that just returns a DataFrame. Once you need more than one output or fine-grained write control, drop to `@transform`.

followups.q: The output is written via out.write_dataframe. What transaction type does a normal build produce, and why care?
followups.a: A standard non-incremental build writes a SNAPSHOT, replacing the entire output view each run. It matters because SNAPSHOT outputs can't be consumed incrementally downstream — every run is full reprocessing. If downstream needs cheap incremental reads, you must make this transform append-only via `@incremental`, not just rely on the default snapshot behavior.

q: What are the four Foundry transaction types and how does each change a dataset view?
a: SNAPSHOT replaces the entire current view with a new set of files and is the basis of batch pipelines. APPEND adds new files but cannot modify existing ones; it is the basis of incremental pipelines. UPDATE adds files and may overwrite existing ones, and DELETE removes files from the view. The view is reconstructed from the latest SNAPSHOT forward, applying each subsequent APPEND/UPDATE/DELETE.
fj: Every build just overwrites the dataset, so transaction type doesn't really matter.
fs: There are four types — SNAPSHOT, APPEND, UPDATE, and DELETE — and only APPEND is truly append-only, which is what makes incremental processing possible downstream.

followups.q: How is the current dataset view reconstructed from a sequence of these transactions?
followups.a: The view is rebuilt from the most recent SNAPSHOT forward: that SNAPSHOT sets the baseline file set, then each later APPEND adds files, each UPDATE adds or overwrites files, and each DELETE removes files — applied in transaction order. So the view isn't one physical table; it's the SNAPSHOT plus the replay of subsequent deltas.

followups.q: Why is APPEND the only transaction type that enables cheap incremental downstream processing?
followups.a: APPEND guarantees existing files are never modified, so a downstream consumer can safely process only the files added since its last run and trust prior results remain valid. UPDATE/DELETE can change or remove already-processed files, invalidating earlier output. The append-only invariant is exactly what lets a consumer extend rather than recompute.

followups.q: A dataset accumulates thousands of APPEND transactions and reads slow down. What's the fix?
followups.a: Many small APPEND transactions create file-count and metadata overhead that degrades read performance. You periodically compact by writing a fresh SNAPSHOT that consolidates the accumulated files into fewer, larger ones, resetting the view to a clean baseline. The tradeoff is that the snapshot resets incremental state, so schedule compaction deliberately.

q: Why does a single UPDATE transaction on an upstream dataset break incremental processing downstream?
a: Incremental transforms rely on the append-only guarantee: each run reads only files added since the last processed transaction. An UPDATE can overwrite existing files, so the previously computed output may no longer be valid and cannot simply be extended by appending. When an input receives UPDATE (or DELETE), downstream incremental transforms lose their guarantee and must fall back to a full SNAPSHOT recompute.
fj: UPDATE is fine; the transform just reprocesses the changed rows.
fs: Incremental correctness requires append-only inputs — an UPDATE on an upstream dataset invalidates already-processed output and silently forces the downstream transform into a full SNAPSHOT fallback.

followups.q: How does the transform detect at runtime that it's been forced into a snapshot fallback rather than running incrementally?
followups.a: The framework exposes whether the current run is incremental (e.g., via the transform/input context), and the input read mode reflects it — a non-incremental run reads the full input instead of just `added`. Senior engineers check this flag and can log or assert on it, so a silent fall back to full reprocessing surfaces instead of just appearing as a slow build.

followups.q: If an upstream UPDATE forces a downstream snapshot, does that snapshot cascade further downstream?
followups.a: Yes — when the downstream incremental transform recomputes as a SNAPSHOT, its own output becomes a SNAPSHOT transaction, which then breaks the append-only guarantee for anything downstream of it, cascading full recomputes through the chain. One mutable input can trigger a wave of snapshot rebuilds, which is why keeping the whole chain append-only matters.

followups.q: How do you legitimately handle late-arriving corrections to data that's already been appended?
followups.a: Rather than UPDATE-ing the source (which breaks incrementality), append correction records and resolve them downstream — e.g., keep an append-only event log and compute the current state with a windowed/last-write-wins function, or periodically snapshot-rebuild a corrected view. You preserve append-only ingestion and push reconciliation into deliberate logic.

q: How do you make a Python transform incremental, and how does it know what is new?
a: You add the @incremental() decorator above @transform and read inputs with a mode such as added so you only process files added since the last successful run; you write with mode 'modify' to append rather than overwrite. Foundry tracks the last processed transaction per input and exposes whether the run is incremental. The decorator orchestrates reading only the new data and appending results.
fj: You filter the input on a date column to grab recent rows.
fs: You add @incremental(), read inputs with mode='added' to get only new files since the last run, and write with mode='modify' to append — Foundry tracks the high-water mark so you never re-scan the whole input.

followups.q: What's the difference between reading mode 'added' and 'current' on an incremental input?
followups.a: `added` returns only the files added since the last successful run — the new delta you process incrementally. `current` returns the full current view of the input. You use `added` for the incremental work and `current` when a step genuinely needs the complete input (e.g., a lookup), accepting the cost of reading everything.

followups.q: On the very first run of a new incremental transform, what does 'added' return?
followups.a: On the first run there's no prior processed transaction, so the framework treats the entire input as new — `added` returns all current files and the run effectively processes everything, then subsequent runs read only true deltas. So incremental transforms bootstrap with a full pass before settling into delta processing.

followups.q: Why write with mode='modify' instead of the default, and what would the default do?
followups.a: `mode='modify'` appends the new results to the existing output (the incremental behavior you want). The default replace/snapshot write would overwrite the entire output each run, discarding previously computed rows — defeating incrementality entirely. The write mode must match the read mode: read `added`, write `modify`.

q: Your incremental transform produced bad output. How do you safely force one full reprocess without permanently disabling incrementality?
a: Bump the integer semantic_version argument on @incremental(). Incrementing it invalidates the prior incremental state, so the next run executes as a full SNAPSHOT, after which subsequent runs resume incrementally. This is the supported way to backfill or recover from a logic change, rather than deleting the dataset or removing the decorator.
fj: Delete the output dataset and rebuild from scratch.
fs: Bump the integer semantic_version on @incremental() — that invalidates the prior incremental state so the next run does a full SNAPSHOT recompute, then incrementality resumes as normal.

followups.q: Why is semantic_version restricted to integers rather than letting you set any version string?
followups.a: Foundry only needs to detect a change to invalidate prior incremental state — it compares the integer to the last-seen value, and any increment triggers a one-time snapshot. An integer makes "did the semantics change?" a trivial, unambiguous comparison; arbitrary strings would invite confusion about ordering. You bump it; the framework reacts to the difference, not the value itself.

followups.q: When should you bump semantic_version versus just letting the bad output get appended over?
followups.a: Bump it whenever the transform's logic or interpretation of history changed, so already-processed data must be recomputed under the new logic — appending more rows wouldn't fix the existing wrong ones. If only new data is affected and history is still correct, a normal incremental run suffices. Use the bump for retroactive correctness, not routine appends.

followups.q: After the semantic_version bump triggers a snapshot, what state must downstream incremental transforms handle?
followups.a: The bumped transform emits a SNAPSHOT output, which breaks the append-only guarantee for its consumers, forcing them to recompute as snapshots too. So a single recovery bump can cascade snapshot rebuilds downstream. Plan the backfill knowing it isn't isolated — downstream incremental chains will also do a full pass on the next build.

q: What is a snapshot_input on an incremental transform and when do you need one?
a: By default incremental transforms expect append-only inputs. Declaring an input via snapshot_inputs (or marking it as snapshot) tells the framework that input is read in full each run and may receive UPDATE/DELETE, without forcing the whole transform back to snapshot. It is used for small reference or lookup datasets that change via overwrite while the main high-volume input stays append-only and incremental.
fj: All inputs to an incremental transform must be append-only, so you can't join a reference table.
fs: You declare the reference table as a snapshot_input — it's read in full each run and can receive UPDATE/DELETE — while the high-volume main input stays append-only and incremental.

followups.q: If a snapshot_input is read in full every run, what stops the output from going stale when only that reference changes?
followups.a: A change to a `snapshot_input` alone doesn't reprocess already-seen main-input data — the incremental run still only re-reads newly added main files. Rows produced before the reference changed keep the old joined values. If the reference must retroactively correct history, you bump `semantic_version` to force a full snapshot rebuild rather than relying on incrementality.

followups.q: Why can you freely add or remove snapshot_inputs without breaking incrementality, unlike incremental inputs?
followups.a: Because the framework keeps no per-transaction high-water mark for snapshot inputs — there are no "previously seen versions" to reconcile, they're just read whole each run. Incremental inputs track the last processed transaction, so adding/removing one changes what "new" means and invalidates state. Snapshot inputs carry no such state, so they're safe to change.

followups.q: When would you avoid snapshot_inputs and instead make the reference dataset itself incremental?
followups.a: When the reference grows large, reading it in full each run becomes the dominant cost and defeats the point of incrementality. If the reference is append-only (e.g., an ever-growing dimension log), treat it as a normal incremental input so you read only its new rows too. Reserve `snapshot_inputs` for small, overwrite-style lookup tables.

q: What does setting strict_append=True (and v2_semantics) on @incremental enforce?
a: With strict_append=True and incremental inputs, the output write uses a true Foundry APPEND transaction, so attempting to overwrite any existing file raises an exception instead of silently degrading. This guarantees the pipeline stays genuinely append-only end to end. Palantir also recommends v2_semantics=True for incremental transforms, which is the current default-recommended behavior.
fj: strict_append just makes it a bit faster.
fs: strict_append=True enforces a true APPEND transaction and raises an exception if anything tries to overwrite an existing file — it's a correctness guardrail, not a performance toggle — and Palantir also recommends v2_semantics=True for future compatibility.

followups.q: What happens to strict_append behavior if the input datasets aren't actually incremental on a given run?
followups.a: If the inputs are non-incremental (e.g., the run falls back to snapshot), `strict_append=True` runs as a SNAPSHOT instead of erroring. To truly guarantee an incremental APPEND path, pair it with `require_incremental=True`, which forces the run to fail rather than silently snapshot when incrementality is unavailable.

followups.q: How does strict_append differ from just writing with mode='modify'?
followups.a: `mode='modify'` appends logically but still permits overwriting existing files, so a buggy write can corrupt prior data without complaint. `strict_append=True` enforces the underlying transaction is a true APPEND, raising an exception on any overwrite attempt. It's the difference between "intend to append" and "the platform refuses anything but append."

followups.q: The docs say v2 and v1 semantics behave identically — so why bother recommending v2_semantics=True?
followups.a: It future-proofs the transform: v2 is the maintained path, so new incremental features, fixes, and the eventual default land there. Setting it now avoids a behavioral surprise when v1 is deprecated and ensures you're on the codepath Palantir actively supports. There's no runtime downside since behavior matches today.

q: What is a Code Repository in Foundry and what role do CI checks play?
a: A Code Repository is a Git-backed environment where transforms (Python, Java, SQL) are version-controlled, branched, and reviewed via pull requests. Continuous integration checks run on each commit: they validate the code compiles, run unit tests, and resolve the declared inputs and outputs so Foundry can register the transform in the build graph. Merging to the default branch is what promotes the logic to the production pipeline.
fj: It's just an in-browser text editor for Spark scripts.
fs: A Code Repository is Git-backed — you branch, open a PR, and CI validates that the code compiles, tests pass, and declared inputs/outputs register into the build graph before merging to the default branch promotes the logic to production.

followups.q: Does merging to the default branch immediately rebuild downstream datasets?
followups.a: No — merging registers the new logic in the build graph, but the output dataset only updates when the transform next builds, via a schedule or a manual/triggered build. Promotion changes what will run, not the materialized data. That's why a build schedule or trigger is needed for the change to actually flow through.

followups.q: How do CI-resolved inputs/outputs on a feature branch interact with the same transform on Main?
followups.a: On a branch, the transform's outputs are written to branch-scoped dataset views, isolated from Main, so you can build and inspect without disturbing production. CI resolves the same declared `Input`/`Output` paths but against the branch's view. Merging reconciles the logic into Main, after which Main's view rebuilds with the new code.

followups.q: What breaks if two transforms in the repo declare the same Output path?
followups.a: Foundry requires a single producing transform per output dataset; two transforms writing the same `Output` path creates an ambiguous build graph and CI will reject it. The build system needs exactly one owner per node to compute lineage and scheduling. You either merge the logic or split into distinct output datasets.

q: How are Python dependencies (conda vs pip) managed in a Foundry Python transforms repo?
a: Dependencies are declared in the repo's meta.yaml: the conda run/build requirements pin libraries resolved from the configured conda channels. If a library is only on PyPI, you add it under the additional pip section so it installs on top of the conda environment. CI resolves and locks this environment, and malformed version operators (for example a stray space in 'scipy <= 1.4.0') will fail the checks.
fj: You just run pip install in the transform code at runtime.
fs: Dependencies go in meta.yaml — conda run requirements first, then a pip section for PyPI-only libraries — and CI resolves and locks the environment at build time, so nothing is installed imperatively at runtime.

followups.q: Why prefer conda requirements over pip for most libraries in Foundry?
followups.a: Conda resolves a consistent set across packages including compiled/native dependencies, and Foundry's curated channels are vetted and reproducible. Pip-on-top can satisfy PyPI-only libs but won't co-resolve with the conda solver, risking binary mismatches. So conda is the default for solvability and supply-chain trust; pip is the escape hatch.

followups.q: How is the resolved environment kept reproducible across builds and reviewers?
followups.a: CI solves the declared `meta.yaml` and produces a locked environment that's pinned for that commit, so every build and every reviewer's branch gets the identical resolved set. The lock — not the loose `meta.yaml` ranges — is what runs. This prevents "works on my build" drift from a dependency publishing a new version mid-pipeline.

followups.q: What's the risk of leaving an unpinned version range like 'pandas' in run requirements?
followups.a: An unpinned dep lets the solver pick a newer major version on a later re-resolve, which can change behavior or break the transform without any code change. For production pipelines you pin to a specific version (or tight range) so upgrades are deliberate. Loose ranges trade reproducibility for convenience.

q: Why write a unit test for a Foundry transform when CI already runs, and how do you test transform logic?
a: CI validates the build graph and environment, but it does not prove your transformation logic is correct. You factor the business logic into a pure function that takes and returns DataFrames, then unit test it with a local Spark session and small fixture inputs, asserting on the output. The thin @transform wrapper only wires Input/Output to that pure function, so tests run fast without touching real datasets.
fj: You don't unit test transforms; you just run the build and eyeball the output dataset.
fs: You factor the transformation logic into a pure function that takes and returns DataFrames, then unit test it with a local Spark session and small fixtures — the @transform wrapper is just the thin I/O wire around it.

followups.q: Why is testing the pure clean(df) function preferable to testing the decorated run() directly?
followups.a: The decorated `run` is bound to real `Input`/`Output` handles and the build context, so invoking it standalone means mocking those handles and a transaction. The pure `clean(df)` takes and returns plain DataFrames, so a fixture and a local Spark session suffice. Keep the decorator a thin wire so the testable surface has no Foundry I/O.

followups.q: What incremental-specific logic can a pure-function unit test miss entirely?
followups.a: It can't exercise the added/modified read modes, the snapshot-vs-incremental branch, or `semantic_version` invalidation — those depend on the framework's transaction state, not the DataFrame math. Pure tests cover the row-level logic; you still need integration builds (or branch builds with crafted transactions) to validate incremental orchestration.

followups.q: How do you keep the local Spark session in tests fast and deterministic?
followups.a: Use a single local Spark session fixture (e.g. shared across tests), tiny in-memory fixtures, and set a low shuffle-partition count to avoid spawning many empty tasks. Avoid wide shuffles and real I/O. Deterministic inputs plus assertions on counts/specific rows keep tests fast and stable versus eyeballing built datasets.

q: What is Data Lineage in Foundry and how is it used operationally?
a: Data Lineage is the graph view of how datasets, transforms, and ontology objects depend on each other, traversable upstream and downstream. Operationally you use it to do impact analysis before a change, diagnose where a stale or broken dataset originated, and check resource permissions across a pipeline. It is the canonical map for understanding what breaks if an upstream input changes.
fj: Lineage is just a diagram you look at; it has no operational use.
fs: I use lineage operationally — to trace upstream to the root cause of a broken dataset, do downstream impact analysis before a change, and verify marking propagation across the pipeline.

followups.q: How does lineage distinguish a column-level dependency from a dataset-level one, and why does it matter?
followups.a: Standard lineage is dataset/transform-grained: it shows that B depends on A. Column-level lineage (where available) traces which input columns feed which output columns, so you can tell whether dropping a column actually affects a downstream field. It narrows the blast radius from "everything downstream of A" to "only consumers of that column."

followups.q: Lineage shows a dataset has no upstream transform. What does that usually mean?
followups.a: It's typically a source/imported dataset — written by a data connection, file upload, or external sync rather than a Foundry transform. It's a graph root, so root-cause analysis stops there and you investigate the ingestion (sync health, connector) instead of an upstream transform.

followups.q: Why is lineage the wrong tool for understanding business meaning of a dataset?
followups.a: Lineage answers structural questions — what depends on what — not semantics. It won't tell you what a column means, whether a join was correct, or if the transform logic is sound. For that you read the transform code, schema docs, and health/content checks. Lineage scopes impact; it doesn't validate correctness.

q: What are markings in Foundry and why are they stronger than project-level sharing?
a: Markings are Foundry's mandatory access controls: a marking (for example PII) grants access only to users or groups on its allow-list. Once applied to a dataset, users without the marking can never see that data even if an owner shares the project with them, and the restriction propagates to every dataset derived from it anywhere in the platform. Project sharing is discretionary; markings are mandatory and inherited downstream.
fj: Markings are basically tags; you control access by sharing the project.
fs: Markings are mandatory access controls — they override project-level sharing and automatically propagate to every dataset derived from the marked one, so a user without the marking can never see that data no matter how the project is shared.

followups.q: If a transform joins two inputs with different markings, what markings does the output carry?
followups.a: The output inherits the union of both inputs' markings — it's at least as restricted as the most restricted input. A user must hold every propagated marking to see the result. This conservative union is why markings can't be "laundered away" by combining data; restriction only accumulates downstream.

followups.q: Can you ever remove a propagated marking from a derived dataset, and how?
followups.a: Not by ordinary editing — propagation is mandatory. Removing a marking requires explicit, governed declassification (a controlled action by an authorized user), reflecting a real policy decision that the derived data no longer contains the sensitive element. Absent that, the marking follows the data everywhere it flows.

followups.q: Markings are all-or-nothing per resource. What do you reach for when access must be per-row?
followups.a: A Restricted View, which enforces row-level security via a policy keyed on user attributes/groups. Markings gate the whole dataset; restricted views filter rows within it. You often combine them: a marking for the broad sensitivity class plus a restricted view for fine-grained row visibility.

q: You must add a PII marking to a mid-pipeline dataset. How do you avoid locking out legitimate downstream users?
a: Because markings propagate to all downstream datasets, applying one can silently revoke access for downstream consumers. Open the pipeline in Data Lineage and turn on marking simulation mode to see exactly where the marking propagates and who would lose access before committing. You then grant the marking to the right groups (or restructure the pipeline) first, then apply it. This impact-first workflow is the senior tell.
fj: Just add the marking; people who need it will ask for access when they hit an error.
fs: Before applying the marking, I open Data Lineage, turn on marking simulation mode to see exactly where it propagates and who loses access, then grant the marking to the right groups first — so no consumer is broken by the change.

followups.q: What if a downstream output legitimately should NOT carry the PII — can you stop propagation there?
followups.a: Yes — insert a transform that strips or aggregates away the PII columns, then declassify that derived dataset so the marking doesn't propagate further. The boundary becomes the point where sensitive fields no longer exist. This is cleaner than granting the marking broadly; you contain it to the segment that actually holds PII.

followups.q: Why grant the marking to groups before applying it rather than after?
followups.a: Applying first creates a window where legitimate consumers are locked out and downstream builds may fail on permission, causing an incident. Granting access first means propagation lands on users who already qualify, so the change is non-disruptive. Sequence the governance change so no consumer experiences a gap.

followups.q: Marking simulation shows a service account would lose access. Why is that especially dangerous?
followups.a: A service account running scheduled builds losing the marking means downstream transforms silently fail or stop producing, breaking pipelines without a human noticing immediately. Service principals must be added to the marking's allow-list before applying, and ideally covered by build health checks so any access regression surfaces as an alert.

q: What is a Restricted View and how does it differ from a marking?
a: A Restricted View enforces row-level security: it sits on top of a backing dataset and a policy determines which rows each user may see, typically driven by user attributes or group membership. A marking is a coarse, all-or-nothing mandatory control over an entire resource, whereas a restricted view filters within a dataset row by row. Note a restricted view cannot be used as an input to a transform.
fj: A restricted view is the same as a marking, just at the row level.
fs: A restricted view enforces row-level security — a policy filters which rows each user sees — whereas a marking is an all-or-nothing control over the entire resource; and restricted views can't be used as inputs to transforms.

followups.q: Since a restricted view can't feed a transform, how do you apply row-level security to data a pipeline must still process?
followups.a: Apply the restricted view at the consumption edge — over the final dataset that apps, the Ontology, or analysts read — not mid-pipeline. Upstream transforms operate on the full backing dataset (governed by markings), and the row-level policy gates who sees which rows at the point of access, not during computation.

followups.q: How does a restricted view's policy actually decide which rows a given user sees?
followups.a: The policy maps user attributes or group memberships to row predicates — e.g., a `region` column matched against the user's authorized regions. At query time the engine evaluates the policy for the requesting user and returns only matching rows. It's enforced server-side, so it can't be bypassed by querying the underlying dataset if the user lacks access to that backing resource.

followups.q: Why might you use a restricted view feeding an object type instead of filtering rows in a transform?
followups.a: A transform filter is static — it bakes one audience's view into the output and can't vary per user. A restricted view evaluates per requesting user at read time, so one object type can show different rows to different users. For multi-tenant or need-to-know access over the same objects, the view is the right layer.

q: What is AIP Logic and what makes it different from calling an LLM API in a transform?
a: AIP Logic is a no-code environment for building LLM-powered functions composed of blocks; the Use LLM block combines a prompt, tools, and a typed output. Its tools are Ontology-driven (data, logic, and action tools) so the model can query objects, call functions, and safely take governed actions through validated action types. Versus a raw API call, AIP Logic is grounded in the Ontology, governed, and testable inside Foundry rather than free-form text generation.
fj: AIP Logic is just a prompt box that calls ChatGPT.
fs: AIP Logic is a block-based environment where the Use LLM block has Ontology-driven tools — data, logic, and action tools — so the model can query objects and trigger governed writeback through validated action types, not just return free text.

followups.q: When an AIP Logic LLM block invokes an action tool, whose permissions apply?
followups.a: The action executes within the invoking user's permissions, not some elevated service identity — the LLM can only request a tool call, and AIP Logic runs it as that user. So a user can never effect edits they couldn't perform manually, and every edit retains attribution through the action type's audit trail.

followups.q: Why does the typed output of a Use LLM block matter for downstream blocks?
followups.a: A typed output constrains the model to return a structured shape (e.g., an object reference or enum), so later blocks like Apply Action or a Workshop binding can consume it deterministically instead of parsing free text. It turns the LLM's response into a contract, which is what makes the function composable and testable rather than brittle string-handling.

followups.q: Given AIP Logic exists, when would you still call an LLM directly inside a transform?
followups.a: For bulk, batch enrichment over large datasets — classifying or summarizing millions of rows during a scheduled build — where you want the result materialized as a dataset, not invoked interactively. AIP Logic shines for live, Ontology-grounded, per-request reasoning with governed writeback; a transform LLM call shines for offline mass processing.

q: How does an AIP Agent (Agent Studio) safely take real actions, and how do you scope what it can touch?
a: In Agent Studio you equip an agent with explicit tools: Object query tools scope which object types it can read, Function tools let it call Foundry/AIP Logic functions, and Action tools let it execute specific ontology edits through action types that enforce validation and permissions. You constrain it by granting only the tools and object types it needs, so writes still flow through governed actions with full attribution. Document context can be added as full text or top-K relevant chunks via semantic search.
fj: The agent has full access to the Ontology and can edit anything the user can.
fs: You equip the agent only with the specific tools it needs — Object query tools for reading, Function tools for calling logic, Action tools for specific governed edits — so it can't touch anything outside that explicit scope.

followups.q: Beyond read/function/action tools, what other tool types can an Agent Studio agent be given?
followups.a: Agents can also have a Request clarification tool (pause and ask the user before proceeding), an Update application variable tool (write into configured app state), and Command tools that trigger operations in other Palantir applications. These let the agent manage conversational flow and integrate with surrounding apps, not just read/act on the Ontology.

followups.q: An agent should never silently mutate data. How do you enforce a confirmation step before an action runs?
followups.a: Gate the action behind a Request clarification step or surface the proposed action in the consuming app (e.g., a Workshop AIP Agent widget) for explicit user approval before applying. Combined with the action type's own submission criteria and validation, the user confirms intent and the edit is attributed. The agent proposes; the human commits.

followups.q: Why scope an Object query tool to specific object types rather than letting the agent read the whole Ontology?
followups.a: Least privilege: a narrow tool surface reduces hallucinated cross-domain reasoning, limits accidental exposure of sensitive object types, and keeps the prompt focused so the model retrieves relevant context. Broad access also enlarges the attack/error surface. Scope to exactly the types the use case needs so behavior is predictable and governable.

q: As an FDE, you must let users edit objects and have those edits persist and flow downstream. How is writeback handled in Object Storage V2?
a: User edits enter through action types, and in Object Storage V2 the Object Data Funnel orchestrates indexing both dataset-sourced data and Action edits into the object databases. OSv2 replaces OSv1 writeback datasets with materialization datasets: object and link edits are materialized into a dataset that keeps the same columns, so downstream pipelines can consume edited state as a normal input. This closes the loop from operational edits back into the data pipeline.
fj: You write the edits straight into the source dataset with an UPDATE so pipelines pick them up.
fs: In OSv2, Action edits flow through the Object Data Funnel into the object databases, and materialization datasets — the replacement for OSv1 writeback datasets — expose those edited states as normal pipeline inputs downstream.

followups.q: A key OSv1-to-OSv2 difference: in OSv2 materializations are optional. What's the implication?
followups.a: In OSv1 a writeback dataset was required to persist edits; in OSv2 the Funnel indexes Action edits into object databases directly, so a materialization is only needed when you want those edits exposed as a consumable dataset for downstream pipelines. If nothing downstream needs the edited state as data, you can skip the materialization entirely.

followups.q: If both the source dataset and user edits feed the same object, how are conflicts resolved?
followups.a: Edits are layered on top of the dataset-sourced values in the object database, so an Action edit to a property overrides the pipeline value for that object until the pipeline itself supplies a newer authoritative value. The Funnel reconciles both sources during indexing; understanding this precedence is key to avoiding edits being silently clobbered by a rebuild.

followups.q: Why is routing edits through the Funnel preferable to having an app write directly to the object DB?
followups.a: The Funnel enforces a single governed indexing path so edits arrive via validated action types with attribution, stay consistent with dataset-sourced data, and become consumable as materializations. Direct DB writes would bypass validation, lose the audit trail, and desync the object store from its backing datasets and downstream pipelines.

q: Describe the end-to-end FDE workflow to ship an operational use case in Foundry.
a: Integrate sources and clean them with transforms in code repositories or Pipeline Builder, then model the cleaned data into the Ontology as object and link types. Add action types and functions for the kinetic layer, build the operational app in Workshop, and layer AIP Logic or agents where useful. Finally wire health checks and build schedules so the pipeline stays fresh and monitored in production. The FDE's job is the whole loop from raw data to a governed, operational application.
fj: FDE work is mostly writing Spark jobs to produce clean tables for analysts.
fs: The FDE workflow is the full loop: integrate and clean data with transforms, model it into the Ontology as object and link types, add action types and functions for writeback, build the operational app in Workshop, then wire build schedules and health checks.

followups.q: Where in this workflow do markings and access governance belong, and why early?
followups.a: Apply markings/restricted views during integration, as sensitive data lands, because controls propagate downstream automatically — model first and you risk PII flowing into the Ontology and apps unprotected. Getting governance right at ingestion means every derived dataset, object, and app inherits correct access for free.

followups.q: What makes the Ontology-modeling step the pivotal part of the loop rather than the transforms?
followups.a: Transforms produce clean tables, but the Ontology turns them into operational objects with links, actions, and functions that apps and agents act on. It's the layer where analytics becomes operational — without it you have a warehouse feeding dashboards. The kinetic layer (actions/functions) is what enables writeback and live workflows, which is the whole point of an operational use case.

followups.q: Why are build schedules and health checks listed as part of shipping, not an afterthought?
followups.a: An operational app drives real decisions, so stale or broken data is an incident, not a cosmetic issue. Schedules keep outputs fresh and health checks (freshness/schema/content) catch silent degradation before users act on bad data. Shipping without them means the use case works once and rots; monitoring is what makes it production-grade.

q: What kinds of health checks should a production Foundry pipeline have, and what does each catch?
a: Use job and build checks to confirm builds complete successfully and within an expected duration, freshness or time-since-last-updated checks to ensure data is current, and schema and content checks (row counts, primary-key uniqueness, expectations) to catch data-quality regressions. Combined with build schedules, these alert on stale, failed, or malformed outputs before downstream consumers are affected. The senior move is monitoring freshness and content, not just whether the job ran.
fj: Just set up an alert if the build fails.
fs: Beyond build-success alerts, you need freshness checks on a load-timestamp column, schema checks, and content checks like row counts and primary-key uniqueness — that's how you catch silent staleness or bad data, not just hard job failures.

followups.q: A build succeeds every night but data is hours stale. Which check catches this and why doesn't a build check?
followups.a: A freshness/time-since-last-updated check catches it — it asserts the latest data's timestamp is within an expected window, independent of whether the job ran. A build check only confirms the job completed successfully, so a build that succeeds while an upstream sync silently stalled looks healthy yet serves stale data.

followups.q: How do build-time data expectations differ from a separate health check, and when prefer each?
followups.a: Build-time expectations run inside the transform and can abort the build on failure, preventing bad data from ever materializing — preventive. A standalone health check evaluates an already-built dataset and alerts — detective, non-blocking. Use expectations for invariants that must never reach downstream; use health checks for monitoring and cross-dataset freshness.

followups.q: Why add a primary-key uniqueness check when the Ontology already keys objects by primary key?
followups.a: Because duplicate primary keys in the backing dataset cause silent indexing collisions — objects overwrite each other or fail to index — and you'd discover it as missing/wrong objects in the app, not as an error. A uniqueness content check catches the duplication at the dataset before it corrupts the Ontology projection.

q: When would you build a pipeline in Pipeline Builder versus a Code Repository?
a: Pipeline Builder is a graph- and form-based, low-code app where data engineers and less-technical users assemble transforms point-and-click, with a built-in release process; it covers most integration and cleaning work. Code Repositories is a git-backed IDE for Python, Java, or SQL, used when you need custom logic Pipeline Builder doesn't express, reusable libraries, or fine-grained control. They interoperate: both read and write Foundry datasets, so a repo-built stage can sit before, after, or in the middle of a Pipeline Builder graph.
fj: Pipeline Builder is for prototypes and Code Repositories is for the real production pipeline.
fs: Pipeline Builder is for integration and standardized cleaning where less-technical users can own it; Code Repositories are for custom logic, reusable libraries, and unit-tested transforms — and they interoperate through shared datasets so you can mix them.

followups.q: If they share datasets, how do you hand off mid-pipeline between Pipeline Builder and a Code Repository?
followups.a: You hand off through a Foundry dataset: one tool writes an output dataset and the other declares it as an input. Because both read/write the same dataset abstraction with proper transactions and lineage, the boundary is seamless — a repo stage can sit between two Pipeline Builder stages with full end-to-end lineage preserved.

followups.q: Does Pipeline Builder support incremental pipelines, or must you drop to code for that?
followups.a: Pipeline Builder supports incremental processing through its own configuration on inputs, so you don't need a code repo just for incrementality. You move to a Code Repository when the incremental logic needs custom transaction handling, `semantic_version` control, or behavior the low-code surface doesn't express.

followups.q: What's a concrete signal that a Pipeline Builder flow should be rewritten in a Code Repository?
followups.a: When you find yourself duplicating the same transform pattern across many flows (needs a reusable library), needing unit tests on intricate logic, or hitting a transformation the visual nodes can't express. Repeated logic, testability requirements, and custom UDFs/algorithms are the signals to move to git-backed code.

q: What are the building blocks of a Workshop application?
a: A Workshop module is composed of widgets, variables, events, and actions. Widgets like Object Table, Filter List, and Button Group render the UI and bind to data. Variables hold state such as the current object selection or a filter value and flow between widgets. Events fire on user interaction (a click, a selection change) and trigger logic, including running Ontology action types to write edits back. Workshop is the operational front-end layer over the Ontology, not a BI dashboard.
fj: Workshop is basically Foundry's charting tool for building dashboards.
fs: Workshop is built from widgets, variables, events, and actions — widgets bind to Ontology data, variables hold shared state, events wire user interactions to effects, and action types write edits back to the Ontology — it's an operational app builder, not a BI dashboard.

followups.q: Workshop is for operational apps. When is it the wrong choice versus a code-based front-end like OSDK?
followups.a: Workshop trades flexibility for speed — it's ideal for Ontology-bound internal operational apps. When you need pixel-level custom UX, a public-facing app, or behavior the widget set can't express, you build with the Ontology SDK (OSDK) in a custom front-end. Workshop covers the common operational case fast; OSDK covers the bespoke one.

followups.q: How does a Workshop Object Table actually stay in sync with edits made via an action?
followups.a: The table binds to an Ontology object set; when a Button event applies an action type, the edit lands in the object database and the bound object set re-resolves, so the table reflects the new state without a manual refresh. The reactivity flows from action -> object store -> bound widget, which is why writeback feels live.

followups.q: Why are events kept separate from widgets rather than wired directly widget-to-widget?
followups.a: Events are an explicit trigger->effect layer (set variable, navigate, apply action), which decouples what happens from which widget rendered it. That lets multiple widgets share effects, lets one interaction drive several effects, and keeps logic centralized and inspectable instead of tangled in each widget's config.

q: How do variables and events wire interactivity together in a Workshop module?
a: Variables are the shared state of a module; for example, a Filter List widget writes its selection into a variable, and an Object Table reads that same variable to scope which rows it shows, so the two stay in sync. Events connect a trigger (button click, selection change) to an effect such as setting a variable, navigating, or applying an action type. You compose interactivity by having widgets read and write common variables and wiring events to drive state and actions.
fj: Each widget manages its own data independently and you refresh the page to sync them.
fs: Widgets read and write shared variables — a Filter List writes its selection into a variable, an Object Table reads that same variable to filter its rows — and events connect a click or selection change to effects like setting variables, navigating, or applying an action.

followups.q: What's the difference between a primitive variable and an object-set variable in Workshop?
followups.a: A primitive variable holds a scalar like a string or selected enum (e.g., `selStatus`). An object-set variable holds a live, filterable set of Ontology objects that widgets bind to and re-resolve as filters change. Object-set variables are what let a filter widget progressively narrow what a table or chart shows without re-querying manually.

followups.q: If two widgets both write the same variable, how do you avoid a feedback loop?
followups.a: You design a clear single-writer or directional flow per variable, and use distinct variables for distinct concerns rather than overloading one. Events should set state deterministically, not in cycles. Where two inputs must affect one result, derive it from separate source variables instead of having both widgets mutate the same one.

followups.q: How would you make one action's success automatically refresh a filtered object set?
followups.a: Bind the table/chart to an object-set variable derived from the Ontology; when the action applies, the object store updates and the bound set re-resolves automatically. If you also need to reset a filter or selection after the action, wire the action's on-success event to set those variables, so the view returns to a clean state.

q: What is a Foundry merge proposal and what do merge checks verify before you merge a branch?
a: Foundry Branching lets you iterate on resources (transforms, Pipeline Builder, Ontology, Workshop) on a branch isolated from Main. A merge proposal is the request to bring those branch changes into Main, analogous to a pull request. Merge checks run automatically and report whether each resource can merge cleanly, surfacing conflicts or errors with links to resolve them. At merge time you choose to build all affected downstream resources or only the modified ones so changes propagate correctly.
fj: You just edit the dataset on Main and rebuild; branches are only for code repos.
fs: Foundry branching covers transforms, Pipeline Builder, Ontology, and Workshop — you open a merge proposal, merge checks validate each resource can merge cleanly, and at merge you choose whether to build all affected downstream resources or only the modified ones.

followups.q: When merging, why would you choose to build all affected downstream resources instead of just the modified ones?
followups.a: Building only modified resources updates the changed nodes but leaves downstream outputs computed against the old logic, so they go inconsistent until their next scheduled build. Building all affected propagates the change end to end immediately, which you want when downstream consumers must reflect the new behavior right away — e.g., a schema change a live app reads.

followups.q: How does branching an Ontology change differ from branching a transform, in terms of risk?
followups.a: A transform change is isolated to branch-scoped dataset views, so Main's data is untouched until merge. An Ontology change (new property, action type, link) affects the shared semantic model; merge checks validate it's consistent, but a careless merge can alter object schemas that many apps depend on. Ontology merges warrant broader impact review than a single transform.

followups.q: What kind of conflict would a merge check surface that a pure git diff wouldn't?
followups.a: Semantic/build-graph conflicts — e.g., the branch and Main both now produce the same output dataset, or a branch references an Ontology property Main deleted. These are resource-level inconsistencies in Foundry's dependency graph, not textual line conflicts, so only Foundry's merge checks (not a git text diff) can detect and link you to resolve them.

q: What block types make up an AIP Logic function and how does it stay grounded?
a: AIP Logic is no-code: a function is a linear chain of blocks such as Use LLM, Apply Action, Execute Function, and Create Variable. The Use LLM block holds a prompt, a set of Ontology-driven tools (data, logic, action), and a typed output. Crucially the LLM never touches tools directly; it can only request a tool call, which AIP Logic executes within the invoking user's permissions. The Apply Action block can call an action type deterministically, bypassing the LLM for precise, fast writeback.
fj: AIP Logic just sends your prompt to an LLM and returns whatever text it produces.
fs: AIP Logic is a chain of blocks — Use LLM, Apply Action, Execute Function, Create Variable — where the LLM can only request a tool call and AIP Logic executes it under the invoking user's permissions, and the Apply Action block can fire an action type deterministically without going through the LLM at all.

followups.q: Why use a deterministic Apply Action block instead of giving the LLM an action tool to call?
followups.a: When the action and its parameters are already known from prior blocks, a deterministic Apply Action is faster, cheaper, and can't be misfired by the model — there's no risk of the LLM choosing wrong parameters or skipping the call. Reserve LLM-invoked action tools for cases where the model must decide whether and how to act based on reasoning.

followups.q: The chain is linear. How do you handle conditional logic that isn't just a straight line of blocks?
followups.a: You use condition/branching blocks and intermediate variables: a Use LLM or Execute Function block produces a typed value, a conditional block routes on it, and Create Variable holds state across steps. So while the overall structure is a controlled flow, you compose branches and variables rather than relying on the LLM to manage control flow itself.

followups.q: How would you unit-test an AIP Logic function before publishing it?
followups.a: AIP Logic provides in-platform test runs where you supply sample inputs and inspect each block's output, the tool calls the LLM requested, and the final typed result. You iterate on the prompt and tool wiring against representative cases, validating grounding and action behavior inside Foundry — before publishing the version that agents or Workshop will call.

q: How does retrieval context in AIP Agent Studio differ from the agent's tools, and what context types exist?
a: Retrieval context runs deterministically on every user message and injects retrieved information into the prompt before the LLM reasons; tools are invoked only when the LLM chooses to call them. Ontology context can supply a fixed set of objects or semantically search for the top-K most relevant objects when the object type has a vector embedding property. Document context semantically searches documents and returns the top-K most relevant chunks. For bespoke logic, custom retrieval functions in TypeScript let you control retrieval per query.
fj: Retrieval context and tools are the same thing — the agent just looks data up when needed.
fs: Retrieval context fires deterministically on every message before the LLM sees anything — it does a top-K semantic search over Ontology objects or document chunks and injects the results — whereas tools are only invoked if the LLM decides to call them.

followups.q: Semantic Ontology retrieval needs a vector embedding property. What does that mean for modeling?
followups.a: The object type must carry a vector embedding property — typically generated by an embedding model over the text you want searchable — so top-K semantic search can rank objects by similarity to the query. Without it you can only supply a fixed set of objects, not search. So enabling semantic retrieval is a modeling decision made upstream, not just an agent toggle.

followups.q: When would you prefer a custom TypeScript retrieval function over built-in Ontology/document context?
followups.a: When retrieval needs logic the built-ins don't express — hybrid filters, multi-step lookups, query rewriting, or combining several sources with custom ranking. A custom retrieval function lets you control exactly what context is fetched per query in code, while still running deterministically before the LLM, unlike a tool the model might or might not call.

followups.q: Since retrieval runs on every message and injects into the prompt, what's the failure mode of over-retrieving?
followups.a: Stuffing too many objects/chunks bloats the prompt, dilutes the relevant signal, raises token cost and latency, and can push key facts past the model's attention. You tune top-K and chunk size to inject only what's needed. Retrieval being always-on makes restraint important — more context isn't automatically better grounding.

q: A dataset deep in your pipeline went stale and you must find the blast radius fast. How do Data Health and Data Lineage work together?
a: Configure health checks on the dataset (freshness on a load-timestamp column, plus schema and content checks); in a Code Repository these can run as build-time data expectations that abort the build on failure. In Data Lineage you color datasets by health-check status to see the failing node and trace downstream consumers that inherit the staleness, and trace upstream to the root cause. Monitoring views then scale this with scope-based rules so new resources get coverage automatically rather than per-dataset setup.
fj: Look at each downstream dataset one by one to see which ones are out of date.
fs: I put freshness, schema, and content checks on the dataset — or build-time expectations that abort the build on failure — then use Data Lineage's health-status coloring to see the failing node and trace upstream to the root and downstream to the blast radius, with monitoring views to cover new resources automatically.

followups.q: Why do monitoring views with scope-based rules beat configuring a health check per dataset?
followups.a: Per-dataset checks don't cover datasets created after you set them up, so coverage rots as the pipeline grows. A monitoring view applies rules by scope (e.g., a project or folder), so any new resource inheriting that scope is automatically monitored. It's policy-based coverage versus manual, drift-prone, per-node setup.

followups.q: The failing node's freshness check is green but downstream is stale. Where do you look?
followups.a: Staleness can originate from a node between them — check freshness along the lineage path, not just the endpoints. Also verify the downstream build actually ran (a stalled schedule or failed service-account permission) and that the freshness check keys off the right timestamp column. Lineage coloring narrows it; you walk the path to the first node that's actually behind.

followups.q: How does using a build-time data expectation change the blast radius versus a passive health check?
followups.a: A build-time expectation aborts the build on failure, so bad data never materializes and downstream consumers keep their last-good version — the blast radius is contained to a halted build you investigate. A passive health check lets the bad data flow downstream and merely alerts, so the radius is everything that consumed it before you reacted.

q: How do Functions on Objects (Ontology functions) differ from a transform, and when do you use them?
a: Transforms run in batch builds to produce datasets that materialize the Ontology; they are not invoked at interaction time. Functions on Objects are TypeScript functions that operate over Ontology objects and links at query time, returning computed values or driving action logic, and they are callable from Workshop, AIP Logic, and agents. Use transforms for heavy batch shaping of data, and functions for on-demand, per-object computation, validation, and logic the operational app needs live.
fj: Functions are just transforms that run a bit faster.
fs: Transforms run in batch builds to produce datasets; Ontology functions are TypeScript that executes at query time over live objects and links, returning computed values that Workshop, AIP Logic, and agents can call on demand.

followups.q: A computed value is expensive and reused across many app loads. Function-on-objects or precompute in a transform?
followups.a: If it's expensive and the inputs change only at batch cadence, precompute it as a property in a transform so every read is a cheap lookup, not a recomputation. Reserve functions for values that depend on live inputs (current selection, user, just-edited state) where a stale precomputed column would be wrong. Trade compute-once-store against compute-per-request.

followups.q: Why are Functions on Objects the right place for action validation logic rather than a transform?
followups.a: Action validation must run at submit time against current object state and the user's parameters — a batch transform runs hours earlier and can't gate an interactive edit. Functions execute at query/interaction time over the live Ontology, so they can enforce submission criteria and compute derived checks exactly when the user attempts the action.

followups.q: What constrains how heavy a Function on Objects can be, given it runs at interaction time?
followups.a: It runs synchronously in the request path, so it's bounded by interactive latency and the object set it traverses — a function scanning huge object sets or doing heavy aggregation will time out or feel sluggish. Keep functions to targeted per-object/per-small-set computation; push large-scale aggregation back into transforms that materialize the result.

q: How do you enable Semantic Search over Foundry Ontology objects, and what must the object type carry?
a: To enable Semantic Search over an object type you must add a vector embedding property to it — a fixed-dimension float array generated by running the object's text fields through an embedding model (e.g., a text-embedding-3 style model exposed via AIP). The embedding transform computes this array and writes it back as a property. Once the vector property is present, Agent Studio's Ontology context can run top-K Semantic Search at query time, retrieving the most similar objects to the user's query by cosine similarity rather than exact-match filtering.
fj: Semantic Search on objects just means a fuzzy LIKE query on string properties.
fs: To enable Semantic Search you must add a vector embedding property to the object type — a fixed-dimension float array generated by running text fields through an embedding model — and Foundry's ANN engine indexes it for top-K cosine similarity retrieval.

code.label: Embedding transform (conceptual)
lines: from transforms.api import transform, Input, Output

followups.q: Why must the embedding be recomputed whenever the source text changes, and how do you automate that?
followups.a: Semantic Search ranks by vector similarity, so a stale embedding drifts from the object's current text and returns wrong top-K results. You keep the embedding in sync by making the embedding transform incremental: it processes only newly changed rows (mode='added') and appends updated vectors, so the property stays fresh without a full recompute.

followups.q: Can an object type support both exact-filter queries and Semantic Search simultaneously in Agent Studio?
followups.a: Yes — the Ontology context in Agent Studio lets you combine a structured filter (on indexed properties) with a top-K Semantic Search on the vector property in the same retrieval step. The filter narrows the candidate set and Semantic Search ranks within it, giving hybrid precision-and-recall retrieval rather than choosing one mode.

followups.q: What dimension should you choose for the vector embedding property, and does it affect index performance?
followups.a: Choose the dimension your embedding model outputs — mismatching the stored dimension causes indexing failures. Higher dimensions improve recall but increase storage and query latency for the ANN index Foundry maintains. As of early 2026 most Palantir deployments use 768- or 1536-dimension models; profile your latency requirements and pick the smallest dimension that meets your accuracy threshold.

q: How does AIP Agent Studio use Semantic Search to populate Ontology context on every message?
a: When you configure Ontology context for an agent and enable Semantic Search retrieval, AIP executes a top-K vector similarity query against the embedding property on the target object type every time a user message arrives. It injects the K most semantically similar objects as grounding context into the prompt before the LLM responds. This runs deterministically and pre-LLM, meaning the model never decides whether to search — Semantic Search fires on every turn, ensuring the agent always has relevant object context without relying on the model to request it.
fj: The agent calls Semantic Search as a tool whenever it thinks it needs it.
fs: When Ontology context has Semantic Search enabled, AIP runs a top-K vector similarity query against the object type's embedding property on every user message — before the LLM sees anything — so the model always reasons over grounded context rather than deciding whether to fetch.

followups.q: How do you prevent Semantic Search retrieval from injecting irrelevant objects when the user's message is off-topic?
followups.a: Combine Semantic Search with a structured pre-filter on object properties (e.g., only objects of a certain status or region) to restrict the candidate space before vector ranking. You can also tune K downward and set a minimum similarity threshold so low-confidence matches are dropped. A tight candidate set ensures low-relevance queries return few or no objects rather than polluting the prompt.

followups.q: If Semantic Search always fires, why does token cost matter more than with tool-based retrieval?
followups.a: Tool-based retrieval is LLM-invoked and skipped when the model judges it unnecessary; always-on Semantic Search injects context on every message regardless. Even for short conversational turns that need no object context, you pay for K objects in the prompt. Tune K and chunk size tightly, and consider conditional retrieval via a custom TypeScript retrieval function that gates Semantic Search on query intent.

followups.q: Can you run Semantic Search against multiple object types in the same agent, and how does context ordering work?
followups.a: Yes — you can add multiple Ontology context blocks, each targeting a different object type with its own Semantic Search. Each block fetches its own top-K; the results are concatenated in declaration order into the context window. Ordering matters: place the most critical object type first so it appears closer to the query in the prompt and gets stronger attention.

q: Describe how to build a RAG pipeline over Foundry documents using Semantic Search in AIP Agent Studio.
a: Author a Document Retrieval context block in Agent Studio and point it at a Foundry media set or document collection. Under the hood, AIP indexes document chunks as vector embeddings. On each user message, Semantic Search ranks chunks by similarity to the query and injects the top-K most relevant passages as grounding context before the LLM responds. This is RAG inside Foundry: no external vector store, no separate orchestration — the chunk store, the embedding index, and the Semantic Search retrieval are all managed by AIP. You control K and chunk size, and can combine document RAG with Ontology context in the same agent.
fj: You paste the entire document into the system prompt so the model can answer questions about it.
fs: You add a Document context block pointing at a Foundry media set — AIP handles chunking and indexing internally — and on every user message it runs a top-K Semantic Search over the chunks and injects the most relevant ones as grounding context, no external vector DB needed.

followups.q: When should you use document Semantic Search retrieval versus loading the document as full-text context?
followups.a: Full-text context suits short, stable documents where every passage is relevant and the whole document fits in the context window. Semantic Search retrieval is necessary for large document corpora or long individual documents where loading everything would exceed token limits or dilute relevance — you fetch only the top-K chunks that match the query, keeping context focused and cost low.

followups.q: How does document chunking strategy affect Semantic Search recall quality?
followups.a: Chunks that are too small lose surrounding context so the embedding doesn't capture enough semantic meaning; too large and a single chunk covers multiple topics, diluting its vector and causing Semantic Search to retrieve partially relevant content. Overlapping fixed-size chunks (e.g., 512 tokens with 128-token overlap) are the practical default; for structured docs, splitting at logical boundaries (headers, sections) improves coherence.

followups.q: How would you evaluate whether Semantic Search is retrieving the right chunks for a given query?
followups.a: Build a small eval set of query–expected-chunk pairs and measure recall@K: how often the correct chunk appears in the top-K results. Use AIP's in-platform function testing to inspect what each retrieval call returns, compare with ground-truth, and tune K and chunk size. Retrieval quality is a separate concern from generation quality — low recall means the LLM never had the right context regardless of prompt quality.

q: What is a vector embedding property on a Foundry object type, and how does it enable Semantic Search over the Ontology?
a: A vector embedding property stores a fixed-dimension float array (a dense vector representation) computed by running one or more text properties through an embedding model. It is a first-class Ontology property type, indexed by Foundry's ANN (approximate nearest-neighbor) engine. When an agent or AIP Logic function invokes Semantic Search, it embeds the user's query into the same vector space and retrieves the top-K objects whose embedding vectors are closest by cosine similarity. This turns the Ontology into a semantically searchable knowledge base, not just a structured filter system.
fj: A vector property is just an array column you can do math on in a transform.
fs: A vector embedding property is ANN-indexed as a first-class Ontology property — not just an array column you run batch math on — so it powers live top-K cosine similarity retrieval at query time.

followups.q: Why index the vector property in an ANN engine rather than doing exact cosine similarity at query time?
followups.a: Exact cosine similarity over millions of objects requires a full scan on every query, which is prohibitively slow at interactive latency. ANN indexes (e.g., HNSW or IVF variants) trade a small recall penalty for sub-millisecond approximate retrieval at scale. For Semantic Search use cases, approximate top-K with high recall is almost always acceptable and is the only practical option at Ontology scale.

followups.q: If you switch embedding models, what must you do to the vector properties across all indexed objects?
followups.a: You must recompute every object's embedding under the new model and reindex — embeddings from different models live in incompatible vector spaces, so mixing old and new vectors causes Semantic Search to return nonsensical results. Plan model migrations as a full backfill: bump semantic_version on the embedding transform to force a snapshot recompute, then let the Ontology index rebuild before routing queries to the new vectors.

followups.q: Can you store embeddings of multiple fields (title + body) in one vector property, and how?
followups.a: Yes — concatenate the fields before embedding (e.g., 'title: {title}\n{body}'), so a single embedding captures multi-field semantics. Alternatively, maintain separate vector properties per field and run multi-vector Semantic Search, combining scores at retrieval time. The single-concatenated approach is simpler; multi-vector is worth the complexity when field-specific ranking matters.

q: How do you configure Semantic Search over objects vs. documents differently in AIP Agent Studio?
a: For Ontology objects, you add an Ontology context block, select the target object type, choose Semantic Search retrieval, and set top-K — this requires the object type to have a vector embedding property already indexed. For documents, you add a Document context block pointing at a Foundry media set; AIP handles chunking and indexing internally without needing a manually modeled vector property. Both block types run Semantic Search pre-LLM on every user message, but the modeling burden differs: objects require an explicit upstream embedding pipeline, documents are self-contained in the AIP document context.
fj: You configure Semantic Search the same way whether you're searching objects or documents.
fs: Ontology Semantic Search requires a modeled vector embedding property on the object type; Document Semantic Search is self-contained in AIP's document indexing — different modeling burden despite the same top-K retrieval mechanism.

followups.q: When would you use object Semantic Search over document Semantic Search even when the source is a text document?
followups.a: When the content is already structured as Ontology objects (e.g., incident reports with typed properties, links to related objects, and action types) and you need mixed retrieval — combine semantic similarity with structured filters or link traversal. Document context returns raw text chunks; object context returns first-class Ontology objects you can filter, traverse links on, and pass to action tools. Choose objects when the downstream step needs structured data, not just retrieved text.

followups.q: Can object Semantic Search and document Semantic Search run in the same agent simultaneously?
followups.a: Yes — you can stack multiple context blocks in one agent: an Ontology context block doing Semantic Search over object types and a Document context block doing Semantic Search over a media set.
