Both run on every message and their results are concatenated into the prompt. Use this for agents that need both structured operational context (objects) and unstructured reference material (runbooks, docs) in one response.

followups.q: How does the embedding model used for object Semantic Search get governed in an enterprise Palantir deployment?
followups.a: In enterprise deployments, embedding models are exposed through AIP's governed model registry — you reference a specific versioned model RID in your transform, and access is controlled by standard Foundry markings and permissions. This ensures the model used for Semantic Search has been vetted by the platform team and that PII-touching embeddings can't be sent to ungoverned external endpoints.

q: You need Semantic Search over a custom-ranked blend of object types. How do you implement hybrid retrieval using a TypeScript retrieval function in Agent Studio?
a: Create a custom TypeScript retrieval function in Agent Studio and use the Ontology SDK (OSDK) to run Semantic Search queries against each object type independently, then merge and re-rank the results (e.g., reciprocal rank fusion or score normalization). Return the merged list as context. This fires deterministically pre-LLM like the built-in context blocks, but gives you full control over multi-type Semantic Search, score weighting, and filtering logic that the built-in UI can't express.
fj: You can only run Semantic Search against one object type per agent; use multiple agents if you need more.
fs: You write a custom TypeScript retrieval function that calls semanticSearch on each object type in parallel and blends them with reciprocal rank fusion — the built-in context blocks aren't the ceiling, you can go fully custom.

followups.q: What is reciprocal rank fusion and why is it a practical default for blending Semantic Search results from multiple object types?
followups.a: Reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) combines ranked lists by summing 1/(rank + constant) for each item across lists, rewarding items that appear consistently high across multiple Semantic Search results. It's score-agnostic — no need to normalize cosine similarities from different embedding spaces — and empirically robust. It outperforms simple score averaging when the vector spaces aren't calibrated against each other.

followups.q: How would you add a structured pre-filter to a Semantic Search call in the TypeScript retrieval function?
followups.a: Use the OSDK filter API to narrow the candidate object set before the Semantic Search vector ranking — e.g., filter Incidents to status='OPEN' before calling semanticSearch. This reduces the ANN search space to relevant candidates and prevents closed or archived objects from surfacing in the top-K, improving both precision and performance.

followups.q: A custom retrieval function runs pre-LLM on every message. How do you keep its latency acceptable under load?
followups.a: Run multiple Semantic Search queries in parallel (Promise.all), set conservative top-K values per type, and apply structured pre-filters to reduce ANN search scope. Cache stable lookups (e.g., a small reference object set) across calls. Profile using AIP's built-in test runner to measure retrieval latency before deploying, and set a timeout so a slow call fails fast rather than blocking the LLM response.

q: How does Foundry's Ontology SDK (OSDK) expose Semantic Search to external applications built outside Foundry?
a: The TypeScript and Python OSDK clients expose a semanticSearch method on object type handles. You pass a query string and top-K, and the SDK calls Foundry's AIP-backed vector search endpoint, returning typed object instances ranked by cosine similarity to the query embedding. This lets custom front-ends (React apps, internal tools) surface Semantic Search over Foundry Ontology objects without building a separate vector database — the index lives in Foundry and is governed by the same markings and permissions as direct Ontology access.
fj: External apps have to copy Foundry data to an external vector DB to do semantic search.
fs: Uses OSDK's semanticSearch method to run Semantic Search directly against Foundry's AIP-backed vector index from external apps, keeping data and governance in one place.

followups.q: What permissions does an external app need to call Semantic Search via OSDK?
followups.a: The app's service token (or the end user's token via OAuth) must hold Viewer or higher access on the target object type, plus any markings applied to it. Semantic Search respects the same Foundry access controls as structured queries — the ANN index is not a separate, ungoverned endpoint. A user without access to PII-marked objects won't get them back in Semantic Search results.

followups.q: How does OSDK's Semantic Search handle multi-tenancy when different users should see different object subsets?
followups.a: Semantic Search executes under the requesting user's identity and respects Foundry markings and restricted views. Two users querying the same object type with the same string can get different top-K results if their access differs. This means governance is free — you don't need to build per-tenant filtering on top of the OSDK call; Foundry enforces it at the index query layer.

followups.q: Can you combine an OSDK Semantic Search result with a structured filter in one call?
followups.a: Yes — OSDK supports passing a where filter alongside the semanticSearch call, so the ANN search is executed within the subset of objects matching the filter. This hybrid mode gives you Semantic Search precision within a structured-filter candidate set, which is the recommended pattern to avoid Semantic Search surfacing irrelevant objects from other domains or statuses.

q: What failure modes arise when Semantic Search embeddings go stale in a production Foundry deployment, and how do you detect them?
a: When the embedding transform falls behind the source data — because a build failed, a schedule stalled, or a semantic_version bump was missed after a logic change — the vector index reflects old text and Semantic Search returns results that no longer match the current object state. The failure is silent: queries return top-K results with high similarity scores that are semantically wrong. Detect staleness by adding a freshness health check on the embedding output dataset, asserting the latest embedding timestamp is within an acceptable window, and wiring an alert to the pipeline owner.
fj: If Semantic Search returns results, the embeddings must be fine.
fs: Stale embeddings are silent — queries still return confident top-K results, they're just semantically wrong; the fix is a freshness health check on the embedding output dataset, not just a build-success alert.

followups.q: Why is a build-success check insufficient to guarantee Semantic Search quality?
followups.a: A build can succeed but produce stale or wrong embeddings if the model was changed without a semantic_version bump, if the wrong input column was passed, or if the pipeline ran but an upstream source was frozen. Build success checks only confirm the job ran to completion; they say nothing about whether the resulting vectors correctly represent the current text. Content and freshness checks are needed to validate the index is semantically current.

followups.q: If the embedding transform is incremental and a batch of objects was accidentally deleted from the source, what does Semantic Search return?
followups.a: Deleted-but-indexed objects can still be returned by Semantic Search if the deletion isn't propagated to the vector index. Incremental transforms process only added rows by default; deletes require explicit handling (e.g., a soft-delete flag plus a transform pass that emits tombstone records to the embedding dataset). Absent that, Semantic Search surfaces phantom objects — results that exist in the index but no longer exist as valid objects.

followups.q: How would you add an automated quality gate that prevents a degraded embedding model from poisoning Semantic Search results in production?
followups.a: Run a lightweight recall probe as a build-time data expectation on the embedding transform: embed a fixed set of known query–object pairs, execute Semantic Search, and assert recall@5 exceeds a threshold. If it drops below the threshold, the expectation aborts the build and the index stays on the last-good version. This catches model regressions, wrong column selection, or embedding dimension mismatches before they reach production.

q: How does Palantir AIP's Semantic Search differ from traditional full-text search on Foundry Ontology objects, and when should you use each?
a: Full-text search matches on exact or tokenized terms — it excels at keyword lookups, identifier searches, and cases where the user knows the exact phrasing in the data. Semantic Search embeds the query and objects into a shared vector space and retrieves by meaning, so it catches paraphrases, synonyms, and concept-level matches that share no literal tokens with the query. Use full-text when users search by known entity names, IDs, or codes; use Semantic Search when users describe a concept or scenario in natural language and the relevant objects may use entirely different vocabulary. In AIP, both can be combined: filter candidates by full-text or property predicates, then rank within that set by Semantic Search.
fj: Semantic Search is just a smarter version of LIKE queries — use it for everything.
fs: Full-text handles known IDs and exact terms; Semantic Search handles natural-language concept queries — and the best approach combines them: filter by full-text predicates first, then rank within that candidate set by Semantic Search.

followups.q: A user types an incident ID like 'INC-20481'. Which search mode should fire, and why?
followups.a: Full-text or exact property lookup: embedding 'INC-20481' in a semantic space produces a vector close to other ID-like strings, not to the conceptual meaning of that incident. Semantic Search has no advantage here and adds unnecessary latency and cost. Gate on input patterns — if the query looks like a structured identifier, route to a property filter; reserve Semantic Search for natural-language queries.

followups.q: In a hybrid pipeline, does the structured filter or the Semantic Search run first, and why?
followups.a: The structured filter runs first — it narrows the candidate pool to objects matching the predicate (e.g., status='OPEN', region='US'), then Semantic Search ranks within that subset. Running Semantic Search over the full object corpus first is expensive and surfaces results the filter would eliminate. Filtering first is both faster (smaller ANN search) and more precise (only contextually valid objects are ranked).

followups.q: Why might Semantic Search surface lower-quality results for very short queries like 'disk space'?
followups.a: Short queries produce low-information embeddings — their vectors sit in a high-density region of the space and are close to many objects, reducing discriminability. Semantic Search performs best with natural-language descriptive queries. For short or ambiguous terms, augment with query expansion (let the LLM rewrite the query into a fuller description) or fall back to a keyword property filter, which handles short exact terms more reliably.

q: How do you govern which users and agents can invoke Semantic Search on sensitive object types in AIP?
a: Semantic Search respects Foundry's standard access control stack end-to-end: the object type's markings gate which users can receive results, restricted views filter rows within the result set, and the OSDK or AIP context block executes under the requesting user's or service token's identity. For agents, scope the Ontology context block to only the object types the agent needs and ensure the agent's service principal holds exactly the markings required — no more. This means a user with PII marking can surface PII-bearing objects via Semantic Search, and a user without it silently gets an empty or filtered result set rather than an access error.
fj: You restrict Semantic Search by adding a custom auth check in the retrieval function.
fs: Semantic Search governance is Foundry's standard access control stack — markings, restricted views, principal-scoped tokens — not a bespoke auth check in the retrieval function; markings and restricted views enforce it automatically at the index query layer.

followups.q: What happens to a Semantic Search result set when the requesting user lacks the marking on some — but not all — objects in the index?
followups.a: Foundry filters the ANN result set at the access layer before returning it: objects the user lacks permission to view are silently dropped from the top-K results. The user gets a smaller or empty result set without an error, and they receive no signal that withheld objects exist. This is the same behavior as structured object queries — access control is enforced, not revealed.

followups.q: If an agent's service principal has broader markings than the end user, which identity governs a Semantic Search call from that agent?
followups.a: In AIP Agent Studio the agent's retrieval context executes under the invoking user's identity by default for user-facing agents — the service principal's markings don't elevate what the end user can see. For fully automated (no-user) pipeline agents, the service principal's access applies. Verify which identity is active for your deployment to avoid accidentally surfacing data the end user shouldn't see.

followups.q: How do you audit which queries were run against a sensitive object type's Semantic Search index?
followups.a: Foundry logs object access events including Semantic Search queries against governed object types, auditable in the platform's activity logs. For compliance scenarios you query those logs to see which principal searched which object type, when, and what their query string was. This is the same audit trail as action writes — Semantic Search reads are covered by the same governance fabric, not a separate ungoverned endpoint.

q: Your org is onboarding a second major business unit onto Foundry. How do you design the Ontology object-type namespace to prevent semantic collision while enabling cross-BU linking?
a: Establish a namespace convention per domain (e.g., 'supply.Order' vs 'finance.Order') enforced by a registered ontology governance council before any object types are created. Separate backing datasets per BU under governed project folders with markings scoped to each domain. Cross-BU links are modeled as link types owned by a neutral integration domain with both BUs as stakeholders, so neither BU can unilaterally mutate the relationship schema. Naming collisions in a shared Ontology cause silent object merge errors and lineage confusion that are extremely expensive to untangle post-facto.
fj: Just let each BU pick names and resolve collisions as they come up.
fs: Before the first object type is created, you lock in a domain-prefixed namespace convention enforced by the ontology council, project/marking isolation per BU, and a neutral integration domain that owns any cross-BU link types — naming collisions in a shared Ontology are nearly impossible to untangle post-facto.

followups.q: What's the minimum governance artifact a BU must produce before a new object type is approved?
followups.a: A type charter: canonical name with namespace prefix, primary key source and uniqueness proof, responsible FDE team, upstream dataset owner, list of consuming applications, and any marking requirements. This document becomes the contract the ontology council signs off on, ensuring no type is created speculatively. It forces clarity before the first line of modeling.

followups.q: How do you retire an object type that two BUs depend on without a coordinated freeze?
followups.a: You never delete a shared type unilaterally. Instead: deprecate the type in the ontology registry (marking it read-only for new consumers), migrate each consumer to the replacement type in a staged rollout with dual-publishing, then remove the old type once consumption is confirmed zero. Run the transition through the governance council so no BU is surprised by a breaking change.

followups.q: Cross-BU link types are owned by an integration domain. How do you handle a situation where one BU's pipeline produces the foreign key the link needs, but that BU goes offline for a quarter?
followups.a: Design the integration pipeline to be resilient to upstream gaps: use an incremental pipeline that carries the last-known link state forward rather than re-deriving from scratch each run, and add a freshness health check alerting on staleness. The BU-offline scenario is a data-availability SLA issue, not a modeling failure. Pre-negotiate SLAs and fallback materialization policies with each BU at design time.

q: You're leading a Foundry delivery where the platform team wants to build every transform in Pipeline Builder and the data engineering team insists on Code Repositories. How do you arbitrate and set standards?
a: Define a capability matrix: Pipeline Builder for integration, standardized cleaning, and no-code patterns the ops team can maintain; Code Repositories for custom logic, reusable library transforms, incremental pipelines with complex semantic_version management, and anything needing unit-tested business logic. Neither wins by default — the question is who maintains it after FDE handoff. Mandate that any Pipeline Builder flow with more than N hand-wired nodes convert to a Code Repository, and gate that via the merge proposal review process. Document the boundary in the project's CONTRIBUTING guide so future engineers don't re-litigate it.
fj: Pick one tool and mandate it across the project for consistency.
fs: Pipeline Builder owns integration and standardized cleaning that the ops team can maintain; Code Repositories own custom logic, reusable libraries, and anything needing unit tests — with a node-count escalation trigger and merge proposal review enforcing the boundary rather than a blanket mandate.

followups.q: What metric signals that a Pipeline Builder flow has grown too complex for the ops team to own?
followups.a: Track: number of nodes, number of hand-configured schedules pointing at it, time ops team takes to diagnose a build failure, and error rate of manual interventions. When any of these cross agreed thresholds, the flow is a refactor candidate. Add a complexity annotation field in the Pipeline Builder project metadata so the platform team can query it.

followups.q: How do you handle the cultural resistance from the ops team who built the Pipeline Builder flows and fear losing ownership when they move to Code Repositories?
followups.a: Co-author the refactored Code Repository with the ops team: they own the business logic review and the merge proposal sign-off, even if an FDE writes the code. Provide a maintained template repo and pair on the first migration. The goal is transferring the knowledge, not the ownership — ops team still signs off on what the transform does; the FDE is responsible for how.

q: At Staff level, how do you design a multi-team Foundry permission model that satisfies least-privilege without creating a support-ticket bottleneck every time a new engineer joins?
a: Model access around role-based groups synced from your IdP (LDAP/Okta) rather than individual users, so onboarding grants come automatically via group membership without a Foundry admin touch. Define a tiered project structure: an integration layer with PII-marked datasets owned by a data-governance group, a domain layer with marking grants scoped per team, and an app layer with viewer access for broad stakeholders. Markings are assigned to the IdP group, not the person. Document a runbook for the three common access patterns (new FDE, analyst, app consumer) so any senior FDE can self-serve onboarding without platform-team involvement.
fj: Grant access when someone asks for it and audit it quarterly.
fs: You model access around IdP-synced role groups, not individual users, so onboarding is automatic via group membership; a tiered project structure scopes markings per domain, and a three-pattern self-serve runbook lets any senior FDE onboard a new engineer without touching the platform team.

followups.q: How do you prevent group-based marking grants from accidentally giving a new hire access to production PII they shouldn't see yet?
followups.a: Split the 'FDE' IdP group into 'FDE-dev' (no PII marking) and 'FDE-prod' (PII marking granted). New hires start in FDE-dev and are promoted after completing a documented PII handling certification and manager approval. The certification creates a paper trail; the split group structure makes it impossible to accidentally land in PII-marked production.

followups.q: A downstream app consumer needs read access to one Ontology object type but nothing else. How do you model this without granting project-level access?
followups.a: Create a minimal-scope service token or user role with Viewer access restricted to that object type's backing dataset (or an object-type-scoped Ontology role if Foundry's IAM supports it), and expose the data via an OSDK-backed API rather than direct Foundry project access. This is the principle of least privilege applied at the object-type grain — the consumer never sees the pipeline or other resources.

q: You are the Staff FDE leading a 6-month Foundry deployment for an enterprise client. The client wants daily fresh data but their source systems expose a bulk extract API that takes 4 hours to run. How do you architect the ingestion and pipeline layers?
a: Layer a CDC-style strategy on top of the bulk extract: run the 4-hour extract nightly off-peak into a raw landing dataset as a SNAPSHOT, then identify an incremental change signal — an updated_at timestamp, an event log, or an API diff endpoint — to power sub-daily micro-incremental syncs throughout the day. Structure the pipeline as: (1) nightly full-refresh landing → (2) daily incremental delta ingestion → (3) APPEND-only incremental merge transform that applies deltas to the golden record dataset → (4) Ontology indexing on the golden record. This gives near-daily freshness on most records without rerunning the 4-hour extract on every cycle. Add a freshness health check alerting if the golden record dataset hasn't received an APPEND in more than 6 hours.
fj: Schedule the full 4-hour extract to run every 6 hours.
fs: Run the expensive bulk extract once nightly as a SNAPSHOT, then power sub-daily micro-incremental syncs from an updated_at signal or change-log API, with an APPEND-only merge layer and a freshness alert if the golden record hasn't received an APPEND in over six hours.

followups.q: The source system doesn't expose an updated_at timestamp. How do you detect deltas without it?
followups.a: Use a hash-based change detection: compute a SHA-256 of the record's key fields in the nightly extract, store it in a prior-hashes dataset, diff against the current extract's hashes, and emit only changed rows to the delta dataset. This is expensive (full scan on the extract) but avoids re-indexing unchanged records downstream. Alternatively, push for the source system to expose a change log — the architectural fix is upstream, not in the transform.

followups.q: How do you handle a nightly bulk extract that occasionally runs 6+ hours and overlaps with the next day's micro-incremental syncs?
followups.a: Gate the micro-incremental syncs on a build-health check of the nightly extract dataset: they only run if the nightly snapshot has a timestamp newer than T-24h. If it's stalled, syncs skip rather than writing a delta on top of a stale baseline, which would produce incorrect merge results. Emit a page-level alert when the nightly extract exceeds a SLA threshold so the ops team can investigate the source.

q: How do you evaluate a build-vs-buy decision on a Foundry use case vs. standing up a dedicated warehouse + orchestration stack for the same workload?
a: Frame it on four axes: (1) Operational vs. analytical — Foundry's Ontology+Workshop+AIP is differentiated for operational apps with writeback; a warehouse+dbt+BI stack is cheaper for pure analytics at scale. (2) Governance surface area — if the workload touches PII across multiple BUs, Foundry's integrated markings, restricted views, and audit trail are worth the premium. (3) Team capability — Foundry requires FDE-specialty talent that is scarcer and costlier; a warehouse stack uses mainstream SQL/dbt skills. (4) Vendor dependency and exit cost — Foundry's Ontology and Workshop lock-in is deep; if requirements are likely to change or the org has strong open-source preference, a modular OSS stack may serve better long-term. Staff engineers drive this decision with a total cost of ownership model over 3 years, not just initial build speed.
fj: Use Foundry if the client already licenses it.
fs: I evaluate it on four axes — operational vs. analytical fit, governance surface area, team capability and talent cost, and vendor exit cost — and build a 3-year TCO model, not just a 'do we already have the license' check.

followups.q: The client insists on Foundry for everything, including a pure-analytics workload that would cost 40% less on Snowflake. How do you advise?
followups.a: Model the 40% cost delta over 3 years, add the FDE talent premium and ramp time, and present it as a written trade-off memo to the client's CTO. Make the recommendation explicit: use Foundry for operational use cases, federate the pure-analytics workload to Snowflake with a read-only data product that Foundry can consume via a connector. Most mature Foundry deployments already use this hybrid — Foundry as the operational/governance layer, not as a replacement for analytical warehouses.

followups.q: What Foundry-specific exit cost should a client understand before committing to an Ontology-first architecture?
followups.a: The Ontology, action types, Workshop modules, and AIP Logic functions are Palantir-proprietary and have no open-source equivalent. The data in backing datasets is portable (Parquet files), but the semantic model, app logic, and governance policies must be rebuilt in another system. Quantify the exit as: re-modeling cost + Workshop/AIP rebuild + retraining. Clients who understand this make an informed bet on Palantir as a long-term platform partner rather than a tool they can swap.

q: As a Staff FDE delivering a Foundry use case for a regulated industry (healthcare or finance), how do you structure your delivery to satisfy both an audit-readiness review and a sub-4-week time-to-first-value demo?
a: Run two parallel tracks. Track A (governance scaffold, weeks 1-2): establish marking taxonomy, IdP group sync, audit log policy, and a restricted-view pattern on PII object types — this is the compliance skeleton that all subsequent work inherits. Track B (value demo, weeks 1-3): build the narrowest slice of the use case on synthetic or de-identified data within the governance scaffold, so the demo can run without touching real PII while already operating inside the compliant architecture. By week 4 you have both a working demo and an auditable pipeline structure — not a prototype that must be rebuilt for compliance. Document every governance decision in an Architecture Decision Record logged in the Foundry project wiki.
fj: Ship the demo first, then add compliance controls before go-live.
fs: I run two parallel tracks — governance scaffold in weeks 1-2 (marking taxonomy, IdP sync, audit log, restricted-view pattern) and the value demo in weeks 1-3 on synthetic data inside that scaffold — so by week 4 the demo is already compliant, not a prototype that must be rebuilt.

followups.q: An auditor asks for a complete lineage trail from a PHI source to a report object. How does Foundry satisfy this, and what must you have set up proactively?
followups.a: Foundry's Data Lineage captures dataset-to-transform-to-dataset chains and action attribution. For a PHI audit you walk lineage from the report object type back to the ingestion dataset, export the lineage graph, and pair it with the marking propagation record and action audit log showing who edited what and when. Proactive requirements: markings applied at ingestion, action types used for all writes (not direct dataset mutations), and health checks that verify the PII marking propagated to every derived dataset. If you built any shortcuts (raw dataset writes, un-actioned edits), the audit trail breaks.

followups.q: How do you handle a client stakeholder who wants to skip the governance scaffold to accelerate the week-4 demo?
followups.a: Make the risk concrete: adding PII markings post-facto on a live Ontology cascades access revocations to every downstream consumer simultaneously, which is an unplanned incident in a regulated environment. Show the marking simulation tool output — the blast radius — as a data artifact. The governance scaffold takes 2 days, not 2 weeks; repairing access after an audit finding takes months. Frame it as risk mitigation, not bureaucracy.

q: As a Principal engineer advising on a multi-year Foundry bet, how do you frame the ontology-first vs. warehouse-first architecture choice for a data org that currently runs on Snowflake and dbt?
a: The warehouse-first bet (Snowflake+dbt) maximizes analytical breadth: SQL-accessible, mainstream talent pool, strong ecosystem, and no vendor lock-in beyond commodity cloud. Ontology-first (Foundry) maximizes operational depth: governed writeback, action-driven apps, and AIP agents that read and act on a live digital twin. These are not substitutes; they address different value surfaces. The Principal-level recommendation is a federated architecture: Snowflake+dbt as the analytical source of truth and modeling layer, Foundry consuming certified data products via connectors and layering the Ontology only where operational apps and AIP need a live, editable model. This avoids replicating analytical workloads in Foundry (where Spark costs exceed Snowflake at scale) and avoids building bespoke operational apps outside Foundry (where the development cost and governance overhead is higher). The risk is integration complexity; the mitigation is a data mesh discipline where Foundry consumes published data products, not raw tables.
fj: Migrate everything to Foundry to have one platform.
fs: Snowflake+dbt wins on analytical breadth and mainstream talent; Foundry wins on operational depth with governed writeback and AIP agents — the right architecture is federated, with Foundry consuming certified data products from the warehouse and layering the Ontology only where live, editable objects are needed.

followups.q: How do you define the boundary between what lives in the Foundry Ontology and what stays in the warehouse?
followups.a: The Ontology models entities that need live writeback, link traversal at interactive latency, or AIP agent grounding. Everything else — historical aggregations, analytics marts, ML feature tables — stays in the warehouse where Spark Compute costs, SQL ecosystem access, and mature orchestration (Airflow/dbt) already excel. The test: 'Does a user or agent need to act on this entity in real time?' If yes, it belongs in the Ontology. If it's a read-only analytical artifact, it belongs in the warehouse.

followups.q: The Foundry contract is up for renewal in 18 months. How do you de-risk the org's dependence on the Ontology before negotiating?
followups.a: Run a migration-cost audit: inventory every Workshop app, action type, AIP Logic function, and OSDK integration; estimate rebuild effort per component on an alternative stack. This quantifies the true lock-in value so the negotiation is grounded in real numbers, not fear. Simultaneously, ensure all data in Foundry is accessible as Parquet datasets (it already is), and document the ontology schema in a vendor-neutral format. You're not threatening to leave — you're negotiating from a position of knowledge.

q: A Principal is asked to set the ontology governance standard for a 500-person data org where 40 FDE teams will contribute object types. What policy decisions must be locked in year one?
a: Six decisions must be locked and documented in a binding governance charter by the end of year one: (1) Namespace taxonomy — domain-prefixed naming convention enforced by CI lint. (2) Primary key immutability — primary keys are surrogate IDs never reused or mutated; business keys are properties. (3) Type lifecycle protocol — deprecation, read-only, and sunset process with an SLA for consumer migration. (4) Marking taxonomy — a canonical set of marking tiers (Public/Internal/Restricted/PII) with clear definitions and an approval process for new markings. (5) Cross-domain link ownership — any link type crossing two domain namespaces is owned by an integration domain team, not either source domain. (6) FDE certification requirement — any engineer who can create or modify object types must pass a documented training and receive a platform-admin grant. Teams that skip any of these produce ontologies that look different but mean the same thing, or silently merge concepts that should stay separate.
fj: Let teams define their own standards and harmonize later.
fs: Six decisions must be in a binding charter by end of year one: domain-prefixed namespace enforced by CI lint, primary key immutability, type lifecycle and sunset protocol, a canonical marking taxonomy, cross-domain link types owned by a neutral integration team, and an FDE certification requirement before anyone can create or modify object types.

followups.q: Two domain teams independently create Customer object types with overlapping but not identical schemas. How do you resolve this at the Principal level?
followups.a: This is a governance failure, not a technical one. Convene the ontology council with both teams and a product owner who can define 'Customer' for the org. Determine whether they represent the same entity (merge with one canonical type) or legitimately different entities (keep both with distinct names and document the difference). Never silently merge schemas — a hidden merge creates data quality issues that surface months later in production.

followups.q: How do you enforce namespace naming conventions at scale without requiring every PR to go to a central team?
followups.a: Implement a CI lint rule in a shared transforms library that validates object type names against the registered namespace registry on every merge proposal. The registry is a YAML file in a governed repo that teams append to via PR — the central team reviews registry additions, not individual object type PRs. This scales governance: central review of the taxonomy, decentralized creation within the approved namespace.

q: As a Principal, how do you make the call on whether to extend the Foundry Ontology to model a new domain vs. recommending a purpose-built micro-service, and what signals tip each direction?
a: Extend the Ontology when: the new domain's entities need to be linked to existing Ontology objects (so link traversal, AIP grounding, and Workshop integration are first-class requirements); the data governance requirements (markings, restricted views, action audit trail) are complex; and the FDE team has deep Foundry expertise. Recommend a micro-service when: the domain requires a custom data model the Ontology can't express (e.g., graph topologies, time-series at tick-level resolution, or hierarchical configs); the team has mainstream software engineering skills but not FDE training; or the interaction pattern is event-driven rather than object-query-driven. The proxy question: 'Would a user or AIP agent need to traverse a link from this domain to an existing Ontology object type?' If yes, model it. If the domain stands alone and only needs to expose an API, a micro-service that publishes a Foundry dataset as a data product is the right seam. Hybrid: the micro-service owns the domain logic, publishes a dataset, and a thin Ontology shell models the entity for linking and AIP use only.
fj: Put everything in the Ontology; it's the platform's source of truth.
fs: The deciding test is: does a user or AIP agent need to traverse a link from this domain to an existing Ontology object? If yes, model it; if the domain stands alone, a micro-service that publishes a data product is the right seam — or a hybrid where the service owns the logic and a thin Ontology shell models the entity for linking only.

followups.q: The micro-service team wants to own its own write path and not route through action types. How do you govern writeback from micro-services into the Ontology?
followups.a: Define a data product contract at the seam: the micro-service writes a certified, schema-validated dataset at an agreed cadence; an FDE-owned Foundry pipeline ingests it and publishes to the Ontology. The micro-service never touches the Ontology directly — it publishes data, Foundry models it. This preserves Foundry's governance (markings, audit trail, action types for user-facing writes) while respecting the micro-service team's autonomy over their data model.

followups.q: How do you evaluate the long-term ontology complexity cost of modeling a new domain vs. the integration cost of a separate service?
followups.a: Project two costs over 3 years: (1) Ontology complexity cost — additional object types multiply governance surface, more FDE training, more merge conflict risk in the shared namespace, and increased AIP context size for agents that traverse the full graph. (2) Integration cost — a separate service requires an API contract, a data product pipeline, and an ownership team that understands both the service and Foundry's ingest patterns. If Ontology complexity cost exceeds integration cost AND the domain doesn't need live link traversal, the micro-service wins. Quantify both; don't decide by intuition.
